import Game.Metadata

World "MeetFin"
Level 23

Title "Boss: Fin Fundamentals"

Introduction "
# Boss: Putting It All Together

Time to combine everything you've learned in a two-part challenge.

**Part 1** ŌĆö *Transitivity through extensionality*: If `x` and `y`
have the same value, and `y = z` as Fin elements, then `x = z`. This
combines `ext` on the goal (Level 15) with `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h`
on hypotheses (Level 7).

**Part 2** ŌĆö *Non-triviality*: Fin 3 has more than one element.
This requires exhaustive case analysis (Levels 16ŌĆō18), existential
witnesses (Level 22), inequality proving (Level 21), and the Fin
contradiction pattern (Level 13) for the impossible branch.

The boss tests whether you can assemble these moves into a
coherent proof. No single level taught all of this at once.
"

/-- Fin equality is transitive through values, and Fin 3 is non-trivial. -/
Statement : (ŌłĆ x y z : Fin 3, x.val = y.val ŌåÆ y = z ŌåÆ x = z) Ōł¦
            (ŌłĆ x : Fin 3, Ōłā y : Fin 3, x ŌēĀ y) := by
  Hint "The goal is a conjunction. Use `constructor` to split it
  into two sub-goals."
  constructor
  -- Part 1: transitivity through extensionality
  ┬Ę Hint "Start with `intro x y z h1 h2`."
    intro x y z h1 h2
    Hint "You need to show `x = z`. Use `ext` to convert this to
    a value equality `x.val = z.val`."
    ext
    Hint "Now you need `x.val = z.val`. Use `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h2`
    to extract `y.val = z.val` from `{h2}`."
    rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h2
    Hint "Now `{h1}` says `x.val = y.val` and `{h2}` says
    `y.val = z.val`. `omega` can chain these."
    omega
  -- Part 2: non-triviality via exhaustive case analysis
  ┬Ę Hint "Perform exhaustive case analysis: destructure `x` with
    `cases`, then split on the value. For each valid case (0, 1, 2),
    choose a different `Fin 3` element as witness and prove inequality
    with `intro h; cases h`. The impossible case (value >= 3) closes
    with `absurd`."
    intro x
    Hint (hidden := true) "Destructure `x`: `cases x with | mk v hv =>`"
    cases x with
    | mk v hv =>
      Hint (hidden := true) "Case-split on `v`:
      `cases v with | zero => ... | succ n => ...`"
      cases v with
      | zero =>
        Hint (hidden := true) "The element is `0`. Provide `1` as witness:
        `use Ō¤©1, by omegaŌ¤®`, then prove `0 ŌēĀ 1` with `intro h; cases h`."
        use Ō¤©1, by omegaŌ¤®
        intro h
        cases h
      | succ n =>
        Hint (hidden := true) "The element has value `n + 1`. Split on `n`."
        cases n with
        | zero =>
          Hint (hidden := true) "The element is `1`. Use `Ō¤©0, by omegaŌ¤®`
          as witness, then `intro h; cases h`."
          use Ō¤©0, by omegaŌ¤®
          intro h
          cases h
        | succ m =>
          Hint (hidden := true) "The element has value `m + 2`. Split on `m`."
          cases m with
          | zero =>
            Hint (hidden := true) "The element is `2`. Use `Ō¤©0, by omegaŌ¤®`
            as witness, then `intro h; cases h`."
            use Ō¤©0, by omegaŌ¤®
            intro h
            cases h
          | succ k =>
            Hint (hidden := true) "Here `v = k + 3` but `hv` says `v < 3`.
            This is impossible: `exact absurd hv (by omega)`."
            exact absurd hv (by omega)

Conclusion "
Congratulations ŌĆö you've completed **Meet Fin**!

You now have these core moves for working with `Fin n`:

| Move | Tool | Learned in |
|---|---|---|
| Construct a `Fin n` element | `Ō¤©k, by omegaŌ¤®` | Level 1 |
| Extract the number / coercion | `.val`, `.isLt`, `Ōåæi` | Level 2 |
| Upper bound proof | `.isLt` + `omega` | Level 3 |
| Combine bounds from multiple elements | multiple `.isLt` + `omega` | Level 4 |
| Prove `Fin` elements equal | `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` or `ext` | Levels 5, 15 |
| Rewrite hypotheses | `rw [...] at h` | Levels 7ŌĆō8 |
| Derive Fin.eta | `rw [Fin.ext_iff]; rfl` | Level 10 |
| Proof irrelevance | `Fin.ext_iff` + `omega` | Level 11 |
| General `Fin (n+1)` reasoning | `.isLt` + `omega` | Level 12 |
| Fin contradiction pattern | `.isLt` + `omega` on empty/impossible | Level 13 |
| Handle `Fin 1` (singleton) | `ext` + `omega` | Level 15 |
| Destructure and case-split | `cases` (nested) + `absurd` | Levels 16ŌĆō18 |
| Modular arithmetic | construction + `rfl` | Level 19 |
| Use Fin as a concrete index | case analysis on indexed data | Level 20 |
| Prove `Fin` inequality | `intro h; cases h` | Level 21 |
| Existential witness | `use Ō¤©k, by omegaŌ¤®` | Level 22 |

**Where this is going**: `Fin n` is the *canonical* finite type in
Lean ŌĆö the standard model that everything connects back to. Every
finite type with `n` elements is equivalent to `Fin n`, making it
the universal representative. A function `Fin n ŌåÆ ╬▒` is essentially
an `n`-tuple: an ordered list of `n` elements from `╬▒`. You'll
explore this idea in the FinTuples world.

Beyond tuples, you'll meet `Finset` (unordered finite collections),
`Fintype` (types with finitely many elements), and big operators
like `Ōłæ` and `ŌłÅ` over finite index sets. All of these are built on
the foundation you've just learned.

(As you saw in Level 19, `Fin n` also carries modular arithmetic ŌĆö
addition and multiplication wrap around mod `n`, making `Fin n` a
model of `Ōäż/nŌäż`. This course focuses on `Fin` as a finite index
type, not as a number system.)

In the next world, you'll learn to **navigate** within `Fin n` ŌĆö
moving forward with `Fin.succ`, embedding with `Fin.castSucc`, and
reaching the boundary with `Fin.last`.
"

1ŌåÆimport Game.Metadata
     2ŌåÆ
     3ŌåÆWorld "PsetFin"
     4ŌåÆLevel 14
     5ŌåÆ
     6ŌåÆTitle "A Cyclic Permutation"
     7ŌåÆ
     8ŌåÆIntroduction "
     9ŌåÆ# Permutations as Functions
    10ŌåÆ
    11ŌåÆA **permutation** of `Fin n` is a bijection `Fin n -> Fin n` ŌĆö
    12ŌåÆa rearrangement of all the elements. Permutations are fundamental
    13ŌåÆobjects in algebra (they form the symmetric group $S_n$) and in
    14ŌåÆcombinatorics (counting arrangements).
    15ŌåÆ
    16ŌåÆThe simplest nontrivial permutation of `Fin 3` is the **cyclic
    17ŌåÆpermutation**: $0 \\mapsto 1 \\mapsto 2 \\mapsto 0$. In Lean, this
    18ŌåÆis the tuple `![1, 2, 0] : Fin 3 -> Fin 3`.
    19ŌåÆ
    20ŌåÆ**Your task**: Prove that this permutation has **no fixed points**
    21ŌåÆŌĆö no element maps to itself. This requires checking all three
    22ŌåÆindices with the case split + inequality pattern.
    23ŌåÆ"
    24ŌåÆ
    25ŌåÆ/-- The cyclic permutation on Fin 3 has no fixed points. -/
    26ŌåÆStatement : ŌłĆ i : Fin 3, (![1, 2, 0] : Fin 3 ŌåÆ Fin 3) i ŌēĀ i := by
    27ŌåÆ  Hint "Destructure `i` and case-split on all three values.
    28ŌåÆ  At each value, the permutation's output differs from the input."
    29ŌåÆ  Hint (hidden := true) "`intro i; cases i with | mk v hv =>`
    30ŌåÆ  then `cases v with | zero => ... | succ n => ...`"
    31ŌåÆ  intro i
    32ŌåÆ  cases i with
    33ŌåÆ  | mk v hv =>
    34ŌåÆ    cases v with
    35ŌåÆ    | zero =>
    36ŌåÆ      Hint "Here `i = 0` and `![1, 2, 0] 0 = 1`. Prove `1 /= 0`."
    37ŌåÆ      Hint (hidden := true) "`intro h; cases h`"
    38ŌåÆ      intro h; cases h
    39ŌåÆ    | succ n =>
    40ŌåÆ      cases n with
    41ŌåÆ      | zero =>
    42ŌåÆ        Hint "Here `i = 1` and `![1, 2, 0] 1 = 2`. Prove `2 /= 1`."
    43ŌåÆ        Hint (hidden := true) "`intro h; cases h`"
    44ŌåÆ        intro h; cases h
    45ŌåÆ      | succ m =>
    46ŌåÆ        cases m with
    47ŌåÆ        | zero =>
    48ŌåÆ          Hint "Here `i = 2` and `![1, 2, 0] 2 = 0`. Prove `0 /= 2`."
    49ŌåÆ          Hint (hidden := true) "`intro h; cases h`"
    50ŌåÆ          intro h; cases h
    51ŌåÆ        | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)
    52ŌåÆ
    53ŌåÆConclusion "
    54ŌåÆThe cyclic permutation `![1, 2, 0]` sends every element to a
    55ŌåÆdifferent element ŌĆö it has no fixed points. A permutation with
    56ŌåÆno fixed points is called a **derangement**. Derangements appear
    57ŌåÆthroughout combinatorics ŌĆö counting them leads to beautiful
    58ŌåÆformulas involving alternating sums and factorials.
    59ŌåÆ
    60ŌåÆThis is your first encounter with `Fin n -> Fin n` as an
    61ŌåÆalgebraic object. In later worlds, you'll see that:
    62ŌåÆ- Permutations form a **group** under composition
    63ŌåÆ- The number of permutations of `Fin n` is $n!$ (factorial)
    64ŌåÆ- The number of derangements of `Fin n` is approximately $n!/e$
    65ŌåÆ
    66ŌåÆThe tools you already have ŌĆö case analysis, inequality, function
    67ŌåÆextensionality ŌĆö are exactly what you need to prove properties
    68ŌåÆof concrete permutations.
    69ŌåÆ"
    70ŌåÆ
    71ŌåÆDisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num by_cases

import Game.Metadata

World "PsetFin"
Level 14

Title "A Cyclic Permutation"

Introduction "
# Permutations as Functions

A **permutation** of `Fin n` is a bijection `Fin n -> Fin n` ŌĆö
a rearrangement of all the elements. Permutations are fundamental
objects in algebra (they form the symmetric group $S_n$) and in
combinatorics (counting arrangements).

The simplest nontrivial permutation of `Fin 3` is the **cyclic
permutation**: $0 \\mapsto 1 \\mapsto 2 \\mapsto 0$. In Lean, this
is the tuple `![1, 2, 0] : Fin 3 -> Fin 3`.

**Your task**: Prove that this permutation has **no fixed points**
ŌĆö no element maps to itself. This requires checking all three
indices with the case split + inequality pattern.
"

/-- The cyclic permutation on Fin 3 has no fixed points. -/
Statement : ŌłĆ i : Fin 3, (![1, 2, 0] : Fin 3 ŌåÆ Fin 3) i ŌēĀ i := by
  Hint "Destructure `i` and case-split on all three values.
  At each value, the permutation's output differs from the input."
  Hint (hidden := true) "`intro i; cases i with | mk v hv =>`
  then `cases v with | zero => ... | succ n => ...`"
  intro i
  cases i with
  | mk v hv =>
    cases v with
    | zero =>
      Hint "Here `i = 0` and `![1, 2, 0] 0 = 1`. Prove `1 /= 0`."
      Hint (hidden := true) "`intro h; cases h`"
      intro h; cases h
    | succ n =>
      cases n with
      | zero =>
        Hint "Here `i = 1` and `![1, 2, 0] 1 = 2`. Prove `2 /= 1`."
        Hint (hidden := true) "`intro h; cases h`"
        intro h; cases h
      | succ m =>
        cases m with
        | zero =>
          Hint "Here `i = 2` and `![1, 2, 0] 2 = 0`. Prove `0 /= 2`."
          Hint (hidden := true) "`intro h; cases h`"
          intro h; cases h
        | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)

Conclusion "
The cyclic permutation `![1, 2, 0]` sends every element to a
different element ŌĆö it has no fixed points. A permutation with
no fixed points is called a **derangement**. Derangements appear
throughout combinatorics ŌĆö counting them leads to beautiful
formulas involving alternating sums and factorials.

This is your first encounter with `Fin n -> Fin n` as an
algebraic object. In later worlds, you'll see that:
- Permutations form a **group** under composition
- The number of permutations of `Fin n` is $n!$ (factorial)
- The number of derangements of `Fin n` is approximately $n!/e$

The tools you already have ŌĆö case analysis, inequality, function
extensionality ŌĆö are exactly what you need to prove properties
of concrete permutations.
"

file.content:
import Game.Metadata

World "PsetFin"
Level 17

Title "The Pigeonhole Principle (Concrete)"

Introduction "
# Three Pigeons, Two Holes

The **pigeonhole principle** says: if you put more objects into
fewer boxes, at least two objects share a box.

Formally: any function `f : Fin 3 -> Fin 2` must send two
different inputs to the same output. There are 3 inputs but
only 2 possible outputs, so a collision is inevitable.

This is your first real **theorem from combinatorics** ŌĆö and
you can prove it using only the tools from Phase 1.

**Strategy**:
1. Classify each `Fin 2` value: every `x : Fin 2` is either `0` or `1`
2. Apply the classification to `f 0` and `f 1`
3. If they're equal: witnesses are `0` and `1`
4. If they differ: check `f 2` ŌĆö it must match one of them
"

/-- Any function from Fin 3 to Fin 2 has a collision. -/
Statement (f : Fin 3 ŌåÆ Fin 2) : Ōłā i j : Fin 3, i ŌēĀ j Ōł¦ f i = f j := by
  -- Helper: every Fin 2 element is 0 or 1
  Hint "First, prove a helper: every element of `Fin 2` is `0` or `1`.
  Use `have classify : forall x : Fin 2, x = 0 or x = 1 := by ...`"
  Hint (hidden := true) "Inside the `have`, destructure:
  `intro (v, hv); cases v with | zero => left; rfl | succ n => ...`
  For `succ n`: `cases n with | zero => right; rfl | succ m => exact absurd hv (by omega)`"
  have classify : ŌłĆ x : Fin 2, x = 0 Ōł© x = 1 := by
    intro Ō¤©v, hvŌ¤®
    cases v with
    | zero => left; rfl
    | succ n =>
      cases n with
      | zero => right; rfl
      | succ m => exact absurd hv (by omega)
  Hint "Now classify `f 0` and `f 1`:
  `have h0 := classify (f 0)` and `have h1 := classify (f 1)`."
  have h0 := classify (f 0)
  have h1 := classify (f 1)
  Hint "Case-split on `h0` and `h1`. If both are the same value,
  you have a collision at indices 0 and 1. If they differ, check `f 2`."
  Hint (hidden := true) "`cases h0 with | inl h0 => ... | inr h0 => ...`
  then inside each branch, `cases h1 with | inl h1 => ... | inr h1 => ...`"
  cases h0 with
  | inl h0 =>
    cases h1 with
    | inl h1 =>
      -- f 0 = 0, f 1 = 0: collision at 0 and 1
      Hint "Both `f 0` and `f 1` map to `0` ŌĆö collision at indices 0 and 1.
      Provide the witnesses and prove the conjunction."
      Hint (hidden := true) "`use 0, 1; constructor; ┬Ę intro h; cases h; ┬Ę rw [h0, h1]`"
      use 0, 1
      constructor
      ┬Ę intro h; cases h
      ┬Ę rw [h0, h1]
    | inr h1 =>
      -- f 0 = 0, f 1 = 1: check f 2
      Hint "The outputs differ (`f 0 = 0`, `f 1 = 1`), so no collision
      yet. Check `f 2` ŌĆö it must equal `0` or `1`, matching one of them."
      Hint (hidden := true) "`have h2 := classify (f 2)` then case-split on `h2`."
      have h2 := classify (f 2)
      cases h2 with
      | inl h2 =>
        Hint (hidden := true) "`f 2 = 0 = f 0`: use `0, 2; constructor; ┬Ę intro h; cases h; ┬Ę rw [h0, h2]`"
        use 0, 2
        constructor
        ┬Ę intro h; cases h
        ┬Ę rw [h0, h2]
      | inr h2 =>
        Hint (hidden := true) "`f 2 = 1 = f 1`: use `1, 2; constructor; ┬Ę intro h; cases h; ┬Ę rw [h1, h2]`"
        use 1, 2
        constructor
        ┬Ę intro h; cases h
        ┬Ę rw [h1, h2]
  | inr h0 =>
    cases h1 with
    | inr h1 =>
      -- f 0 = 1, f 1 = 1: collision at 0 and 1
      Hint "Both `f 0` and `f 1` map to `1` ŌĆö collision at indices 0 and 1."
      Hint (hidden := true) "`use 0, 1; constructor; ┬Ę intro h; cases h; ┬Ę rw [h0, h1]`"
      use 0, 1
      constructor
      ┬Ę intro h; cases h
      ┬Ę rw [h0, h1]
    | inl h1 =>
      -- f 0 = 1, f 1 = 0: check f 2
      Hint "The outputs differ (`f 0 = 1`, `f 1 = 0`), so no collision
      yet. Check `f 2` ŌĆö it must match one of them."
      Hint (hidden := true) "`have h2 := classify (f 2)` then case-split on `h2`."
      have h2 := classify (f 2)
      cases h2 with
      | inl h2 =>
        Hint (hidden := true) "`f 2 = 0 = f 1`: use `1, 2; constructor; ┬Ę intro h; cases h; ┬Ę rw [h1, h2]`"
        use 1, 2
        constructor
        ┬Ę intro h; cases h
        ┬Ę rw [h1, h2]
      | inr h2 =>
        Hint (hidden := true) "`f 2 = 1 = f 0`: use `0, 2; constructor; ┬Ę intro h; cases h; ┬Ę rw [h0, h2]`"
        use 0, 2
        constructor
        ┬Ę intro h; cases h
        ┬Ę rw [h0, h2]

Conclusion "
You've proved the **pigeonhole principle** for `Fin 3 -> Fin 2`:
any function from a 3-element set to a 2-element set must have
a collision.

The proof strategy was:
1. **Classify** each output as `0` or `1`
2. **Compare** outputs at pairs of inputs
3. **Find** two inputs with the same output

Every tool in this proof came from Phase 1:
- Case analysis on `Fin 2` (MeetFin)
- Existential witnesses with `use` (MeetFin)
- Inequality pattern `intro h; cases h` (MeetFin)
- Rewriting with hypotheses (FinTuples)

The general pigeonhole principle ŌĆö if `f : Fin (n+1) -> Fin n`,
then `f` has a collision ŌĆö is a cornerstone of combinatorics.
You'll prove the general version in a later world with more
powerful tools. This concrete version demonstrates that your
Phase 1 toolkit can already prove real theorems.
"

DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num by_cases
DisabledTheorem funext

import Game.Metadata

World "FinNavigation"
Level 14

Title "castSucc Surjectivity"

Introduction "
# Every Non-Last Element is a castSucc Image

In Level 7, you proved that no `castSucc` image equals `Fin.last`.
This level proves the **converse**: if an element is NOT `Fin.last`,
then it IS a `castSucc` image. Together, they give an exact
characterization: *an element of `Fin (n+1)` is a `castSucc`
image if and only if it is not `Fin.last n`*.

This completes the embedding picture for `castSucc`:
- **Injectivity** (Level 8): different inputs give different outputs
- **Separation** (Level 7): no output equals `Fin.last`
- **Surjectivity** (this level): every non-`last` element IS an output (converse of L7)

Together: `castSucc` is a *bijection* between `Fin n` and
`Fin (n+1) minus the last element`.

**Strategy**:
1. Derive `i.val /= n` from `i /= Fin.last n`
2. Use `omega` to get `i.val < n` (from `i.val < n + 1` and `i.val /= n`)
3. Provide the witness `(i.val, proof)` and prove equality via `ext`
"

/-- Every element not equal to `Fin.last` is a `castSucc` image. -/
Statement (n : ŌäĢ) : ŌłĆ i : Fin (n + 1), i ŌēĀ Fin.last n ŌåÆ Ōłā j : Fin n, i = j.castSucc := by
  Hint "Start with `intro i hne`."
  intro i hne
  Hint "First derive that `i.val /= n`. Use a `have` block:
  assume `i.val = n`, then show `i = Fin.last n` (contradicting `hne`)."
  Hint (hidden := true) "`have hval : i.val /= n := by`
  then inside: `intro heq; apply hne; ext; rw [Fin.val_last]; exact heq`"
  have hval : i.val ŌēĀ n := by
    intro heq
    apply hne
    ext
    rw [Fin.val_last]
    exact heq
  Hint "Now `i.val < n + 1` (from `i.isLt`) and `i.val /= n`
  together give `i.val < n`. Provide the witness."
  Hint (hidden := true) "`use Ō¤©i.val, by omegaŌ¤®; ext; rw [Fin.val_castSucc]`"
  use Ō¤©i.val, by omegaŌ¤®
  ext
  rw [Fin.val_castSucc]

Conclusion "
The surjectivity proof introduced a new technique: **deriving a
value-level fact from a Fin-level hypothesis**. You turned
`i /= Fin.last n` into `i.val /= n` by showing the contrapositive:
if the values were equal, `ext` would force the Fin elements to be
equal, contradicting the hypothesis.

This level and Level 7 together give the **if-and-only-if**:

> `i` is a `castSucc` image **iff** `i /= Fin.last n`

Level 7 proved the forward direction (castSucc images are never
last). This level proved the converse (non-last elements are always
castSucc images). Asking 'is the converse true?' is a fundamental
mathematical habit ŌĆö and here the answer is yes.

With injectivity, separation, and surjectivity, the castSucc/last
decomposition is now fully justified:
- **Exhaustion**: every `i` is either `last` or `j.castSucc` (boss)
- **Disjointness**: no `castSucc` image equals `last` (Level 7)
- **Completeness**: every non-`last` element IS a `castSucc` image (this level)

In algebraic language, `castSucc` is an isomorphism between `Fin n`
and the complement of the last element in `Fin (n+1)`.
"

     5ŌåÆ
     6ŌåÆTitle "Obtain Meets Decomposition"
     7ŌåÆ
     8ŌåÆIntroduction "
     9ŌåÆ# Combining obtain with Decomposition
    10ŌåÆ
    11ŌåÆIn Level 8, you learned `obtain` for destructuring existentials.
    12ŌåÆIn Levels 11-12, you proved general decompositions that produce
    13ŌåÆexistential results (`exists j, i = j.castSucc`).
    14ŌåÆ
    15ŌåÆHere you'll combine them: given that an element is a `castSucc`
    16ŌåÆimage, use `obtain` to extract the preimage, then prove the
    17ŌåÆelement can't be `Fin.last`.
    18ŌåÆ
    19ŌåÆThis is the **converse** of FinNavigation Level 14 (surjectivity):
    20ŌåÆthere you proved `i /= last -> exists j, i = j.castSucc`. Here
    21ŌåÆyou prove `exists j, i = j.castSucc -> i /= last`.
    22ŌåÆ
    23ŌåÆ**Strategy**:
    24ŌåÆ1. `obtain` the witness from the existential
    25ŌåÆ2. Rewrite using the equation
    26ŌåÆ3. Apply the value-reasoning pattern from FinNavigation L07
    27ŌåÆ"
    28ŌåÆ
    29ŌåÆ/-- A castSucc image is never the last element. -/
    30ŌåÆStatement (n : ŌäĢ) (i : Fin (n + 1)) (h : Ōłā j : Fin n, i = j.castSucc) :
    31ŌåÆ    i ŌēĀ Fin.last n := by
    32ŌåÆ  Hint "Destructure the existential: `obtain Ō¤©j, hjŌ¤® := h`.
    33ŌåÆ  This gives you the preimage `j` and the equation `hj`."
    34ŌåÆ  obtain Ō¤©j, hjŌ¤® := h
    35ŌåÆ  Hint "Rewrite `i` using the equation: `rw [hj]`.
    36ŌåÆ  Now the goal is `j.castSucc /= Fin.last n`."
    37ŌåÆ  rw [hj]
    38ŌåÆ  Hint "This is the separation fact from FinNavigation L07, now
    39ŌåÆ  for general `n`. Assume equality and derive a contradiction
    40ŌåÆ  via value reasoning."
    41ŌåÆ  Hint (hidden := true) "`intro heq; rw [Fin.ext_iff] at heq;
    42ŌåÆ  rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_last] at heq; omega`"
    43ŌåÆ  intro heq
    44ŌåÆ  rw [Fin.ext_iff] at heq
    45ŌåÆ  rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_last] at heq
    46ŌåÆ  omega
    47ŌåÆ
    48ŌåÆConclusion "
    49ŌåÆYou combined two skills:
    50ŌåÆ1. **`obtain`** to destructure the existential hypothesis
    51ŌåÆ2. **Value reasoning** (ext_iff + val lemmas + omega) to derive
    52ŌåÆ   the contradiction
    53ŌåÆ
    54ŌåÆThis is a common pattern in real Lean proofs: a previous result
    55ŌåÆgives you an existential (`exists j, i = j.castSucc`), and you
    56ŌåÆneed to destructure it with `obtain` before you can use the
    57ŌåÆequation. The value repackaging goes in reverse here ŌĆö instead
    58ŌåÆof constructing a witness, you're unpacking one that was given.
    59ŌåÆ
    60ŌåÆThe fact you just proved ŌĆö castSucc images are never last ŌĆö is
    61ŌåÆthe converse of the surjectivity result from FinNavigation L14:
    62ŌåÆnon-last elements are always castSucc images. Together they say:
    63ŌåÆan element is a castSucc image *if and only if* it's not last.
    64ŌåÆ"

import Game.Metadata

World "PsetFin"
Level 13

Title "Obtain Meets Decomposition"

Introduction "
# Combining obtain with Decomposition

In Level 8, you learned `obtain` for destructuring existentials.
In Levels 11-12, you proved general decompositions that produce
existential results (`exists j, i = j.castSucc`).

Here you'll combine them: given that an element is a `castSucc`
image, use `obtain` to extract the preimage, then prove the
element can't be `Fin.last`.

This is the **converse** of FinNavigation Level 14 (surjectivity):
there you proved `i /= last -> exists j, i = j.castSucc`. Here
you prove `exists j, i = j.castSucc -> i /= last`.

**Strategy**:
1. `obtain` the witness from the existential
2. Rewrite using the equation
3. Apply the value-reasoning pattern from FinNavigation L07
"

/-- A castSucc image is never the last element. -/
Statement (n : ŌäĢ) (i : Fin (n + 1)) (h : Ōłā j : Fin n, i = j.castSucc) :
    i ŌēĀ Fin.last n := by
  Hint "Destructure the existential: `obtain Ō¤©j, hjŌ¤® := h`.
  This gives you the preimage `j` and the equation `hj`."
  obtain Ō¤©j, hjŌ¤® := h
  Hint "Rewrite `i` using the equation: `rw [hj]`.
  Now the goal is `j.castSucc /= Fin.last n`."
  rw [hj]
  Hint "This is the separation fact from FinNavigation L07, now
  for general `n`. Assume equality and derive a contradiction
  via value reasoning."
  Hint (hidden := true) "`intro heq; rw [Fin.ext_iff] at heq;
  rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_last] at heq; omega`"
  intro heq
  rw [Fin.ext_iff] at heq
  rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_last] at heq
  omega

Conclusion "
You combined two skills:
1. **`obtain`** to destructure the existential hypothesis
2. **Value reasoning** (ext_iff + val lemmas + omega) to derive
   the contradiction

This is a common pattern in real Lean proofs: a previous result
gives you an existential (`exists j, i = j.castSucc`), and you
need to destructure it with `obtain` before you can use the
equation. The value repackaging goes in reverse here ŌĆö instead
of constructing a witness, you're unpacking one that was given.

The fact you just proved ŌĆö castSucc images are never last ŌĆö is
the converse of the surjectivity result from FinNavigation L14:
non-last elements are always castSucc images. Together they say:
an element is a castSucc image *if and only if* it's not last.
"

import Game.Metadata

World "FinsetBasics"
Level 9

Title "Insert is Idempotent"

Introduction "
# Insert is Idempotent

Here's a common misconception: inserting an element always makes the
set bigger. **Wrong.** Finsets have no duplicates, so inserting an
element that's already present does nothing.

The formal statement is `Finset.insert_eq_of_mem`:

$$a \\in s \\;\\longrightarrow\\; \\texttt{insert } a \\; s = s$$

Notice this is an **implication**, not an iff. The direction matters:
- If `a Ōłł s`, then `insert a s = s` (idempotent)
- If `a Ōłē s`, then `insert a s ŌēĀ s` (the set grows by one element)

To use `insert_eq_of_mem`, you `apply` it. This changes the goal from
an equation (`insert a s = s`) to a membership proof (`a Ōłł s`). Then
you prove membership using the `mem_insert`/`mem_singleton` chain
from earlier levels.

**Your task**: Prove that `insert 2 {1, 2, 3} = {1, 2, 3}`.
"

/-- Inserting 2 into {1, 2, 3} gives back {1, 2, 3}. -/
Statement : insert 2 ({1, 2, 3} : Finset ŌäĢ) = {1, 2, 3} := by
  Hint "Use `apply Finset.insert_eq_of_mem` to reduce the equation to
  a membership proof."
  apply Finset.insert_eq_of_mem
  Hint "Now prove that `2` is a member of the set. Peel the inserts:
  `rw [Finset.mem_insert]`, then `right`,
  then `rw [Finset.mem_insert]`, then `left; rfl`."
  Hint (hidden := true) "First `rw [Finset.mem_insert]` gives a disjunction
  `2 = 1 Ōł© ...`. Take `right`. Then `rw [Finset.mem_insert]` gives
  `2 = 2 Ōł© ...`. Take `left; rfl`."
  rw [Finset.mem_insert]
  right
  rw [Finset.mem_insert]
  left
  rfl

Conclusion "
The proof has two stages:
1. `apply Finset.insert_eq_of_mem` ŌĆö reduce the equation to membership
2. Prove membership using the `mem_insert`/`mem_singleton` chain

This is a new proof pattern: **applying a theorem whose premise you
then have to prove**. The `apply` tactic sees that `insert_eq_of_mem`
has conclusion `insert a s = s` (matching the goal) and premise
`a Ōłł s` (becoming the new goal).

In plain mathematics, $\\{2\\} \\cup \\{1, 2, 3\\} = \\{1, 2, 3\\}$ is
obvious because 2 is already in the set. In Lean, you must *prove*
the membership to justify the equality.
"

/-- `Finset.insert_eq_of_mem` states that if `a Ōłł s`, then
`insert a s = s`.

Inserting an element that's already present is a no-op.

## Syntax
```
apply Finset.insert_eq_of_mem
```
This changes the goal from `insert a s = s` to `a Ōłł s`.

## When to use it
When you need to prove that inserting a duplicate element
doesn't change the finset.

## Example
```
-- Goal: insert 2 {1, 2, 3} = {1, 2, 3}

    72ŌåÆ
    73ŌåÆ## When to use it

   391ŌåÆTheoremDoc Finset.not_mem_range_self as "Finset.not_mem_range_self" in "Finset"
   392ŌåÆ
   393ŌåÆ/-! ### FinTuples-specific definitions and theorems -/
   394ŌåÆ
   395ŌåÆ/-- Non-dependent version of `Fin.cons_zero`.
   396ŌåÆ`Fin.cons a f 0 = a` ŌĆö accessing the head of a cons'd tuple returns the prepended element. -/
   397ŌåÆtheorem Fin.cons_val_zero {n : ŌäĢ} {╬▒ : Type*} (a : ╬▒) (f : Fin n ŌåÆ ╬▒) :
   398ŌåÆ    (Fin.cons a f : Fin (n+1) ŌåÆ ╬▒) 0 = a := rfl
   399ŌåÆ
   400ŌåÆ/-- Non-dependent version of `Fin.cons_succ`.
   401ŌåÆ`Fin.cons a f i.succ = f i` ŌĆö accessing later elements of a cons'd tuple returns the tail. -/
   402ŌåÆtheorem Fin.cons_val_succ {n : ŌäĢ} {╬▒ : Type*} (a : ╬▒) (f : Fin n ŌåÆ ╬▒) (i : Fin n) :
   403ŌåÆ    (Fin.cons a f : Fin (n+1) ŌåÆ ╬▒) i.succ = f i := rfl
   404ŌåÆ
   405ŌåÆ/-- `vecSnoc f x` appends element `x` to the end of tuple `f`,
   406ŌåÆproducing a tuple of length `n + 1`.
   407ŌåÆ
   408ŌåÆThis is the non-dependent wrapper around `Fin.snoc` for use with
   409ŌåÆconcrete types like `Fin n ŌåÆ ŌäĢ`.
   410ŌåÆ
   411ŌåÆ## Type
   412ŌåÆ`vecSnoc : (Fin n ŌåÆ ╬▒) ŌåÆ ╬▒ ŌåÆ Fin (n + 1) ŌåÆ ╬▒`
   413ŌåÆ
   414ŌåÆ## Element access
   415ŌåÆ- `vecSnoc f x (Fin.last n) = x` (the appended element)
   416ŌåÆ- `vecSnoc f x i.castSucc = f i` (the original tuple)
   417ŌåÆ
   418ŌåÆ## Example
   419ŌåÆ`vecSnoc ![10, 20] 30 = ![10, 20, 30]`
   420ŌåÆ-/
   421ŌåÆdef vecSnoc {n : ŌäĢ} {╬▒ : Type*} (f : Fin n ŌåÆ ╬▒) (x : ╬▒) : Fin (n + 1) ŌåÆ ╬▒ :=
   422ŌåÆ  @Fin.snoc n (fun _ => ╬▒) f x
   423ŌåÆ
   424ŌåÆ/-- Accessing the last element of a snoc'd tuple gives back the appended element. -/
   425ŌåÆtheorem vecSnoc_last {n : ŌäĢ} {╬▒ : Type*} (f : Fin n ŌåÆ ╬▒) (x : ╬▒) :
   426ŌåÆ    vecSnoc f x (Fin.last n) = x :=
   427ŌåÆ  @Fin.snoc_last n (fun _ => ╬▒) x f
   428ŌåÆ
   429ŌåÆ/-- Accessing an earlier element of a snoc'd tuple gives the original tuple's value. -/
   430ŌåÆtheorem vecSnoc_castSucc {n : ŌäĢ} {╬▒ : Type*} (f : Fin n ŌåÆ ╬▒) (x : ╬▒) (i : Fin n) :
   431ŌåÆ    vecSnoc f x i.castSucc = f i :=
   432ŌåÆ  @Fin.snoc_castSucc n (fun _ => ╬▒) x f i
   433ŌåÆ
   434ŌåÆ/-- Appending the last element to the init recovers the original tuple. -/
   435ŌåÆtheorem vecSnoc_self_init {n : ŌäĢ} {╬▒ : Type*} (f : Fin (n + 1) ŌåÆ ╬▒) :
   436ŌåÆ    vecSnoc (Fin.init f) (f (Fin.last n)) = f :=
   437ŌåÆ  Fin.snoc_init_self f
   438ŌåÆ
   439ŌåÆ/-- `vecSnoc_self_init f` states that
   440ŌåÆ`vecSnoc (Fin.init f) (f (Fin.last n)) = f`.
   441ŌåÆ
   442ŌåÆEvery tuple equals its init snoc'd with its last element.
   443ŌåÆ
   444ŌåÆ## Syntax
   445ŌåÆ```
   446ŌåÆexact vecSnoc_self_init f  -- proves vecSnoc (init f) (f (last n)) = f
   447ŌåÆ```
   448ŌåÆ
   449ŌåÆ## When to use it
   450ŌåÆWhen you need to decompose a tuple into its earlier elements
   451ŌåÆand its last element.
   452ŌåÆ-/
   453ŌåÆTheoremDoc vecSnoc_self_init as "vecSnoc_self_init" in "Fin"
   454ŌåÆ
   455ŌåÆ/-- `funext` is the function extensionality theorem: if two functions
   456ŌåÆagree on every input, they are equal.
   457ŌåÆ
   458ŌåÆDisabled in levels where the `ext` tactic should be used interactively.
   459ŌåÆ-/
   460ŌåÆTheoremDoc funext as "funext" in "Fin"
   461ŌåÆ
   462ŌåÆ/-- `congrFun h a` extracts pointwise agreement from a function equality.
   463ŌåÆIf `h : f = g`, then `congrFun h a : f a = g a`.
   464ŌåÆ
   465ŌåÆ## Syntax
   466ŌåÆ```
   467ŌåÆhave h0 := congrFun h 0     -- extract agreement at index 0
   468ŌåÆhave hi := congrFun h i.succ -- extract agreement at index i.succ
   469ŌåÆ```
   470ŌåÆ
   471ŌåÆ## When to use it
   472ŌåÆWhen you have `h : f = g` and need to deduce that `f a = g a` at a
   473ŌåÆspecific input `a`. This is the **pointwise extraction** move: turn
   474ŌåÆa global function equality into a local value equality.
   475ŌåÆ-/
   476ŌåÆTheoremDoc congrFun as "congrFun" in "Fin"
   477ŌåÆ
   478ŌåÆ/-- `Function.comp_apply` states that `(g Ōłś f) x = g (f x)`.
   479ŌåÆ
   480ŌåÆ## Syntax
   481ŌåÆ```
   482ŌåÆrw [Function.comp_apply]  -- unfolds (g Ōłś f) x to g (f x)
   483ŌåÆ```
   484ŌåÆ
   485ŌåÆ## When to use it
   486ŌåÆWhen you need to unfold a composition `(g Ōłś f) x` to the explicit
   487ŌåÆapplication `g (f x)` ŌĆö for example, before substituting formulas
   488ŌåÆfor `f` and `g` with `rw`.
   489ŌåÆ-/
   490ŌåÆTheoremDoc Function.comp_apply as "Function.comp_apply" in "Fin"

import GameServer
import Mathlib.Data.Fin.Basic
import Mathlib.Data.Fin.VecNotation
import Mathlib.Data.Finset.Basic

/-! ## Documentation for disabled tactics and theorems

These docs are needed by the GameServer when tactics/theorems are disabled.
They are placed here so all level files can reference them.
-/

/-! ### NNG4-baseline tactics

These tactics are assumed known from the Natural Number Game or
equivalent. They are introduced in Level 1 so the game doesn't warn
about missing introductions.
-/

/-- `exact e` closes the goal if `e` has exactly the type of the goal.

## Syntax
```
exact expression
```

## When to use it
When you have a term (variable, function application, constructor)
whose type matches the goal exactly.
-/
TacticDoc exact

/-- `rw [h]` rewrites the goal using an equation or iff `h`.

## Syntax
```
rw [h]           -- rewrite the goal
rw [h] at hyp    -- rewrite a hypothesis
rw [ŌåÉ h]         -- rewrite right-to-left
rw [h1, h2, h3]  -- chain multiple rewrites
```

## When to use it
When you have an equation `h : a = b` or iff `h : P Ōåö Q` and want
to replace `a` with `b` (or `P` with `Q`) in the goal or a hypothesis.
-/
TacticDoc rw

/-- `intro x` introduces a universally quantified variable or an
antecedent of an implication into the context.

## Syntax
```
intro x       -- introduce one variable
intro x y z   -- introduce multiple variables
intro h       -- introduce an implication hypothesis
```

## When to use it
When the goal is `ŌłĆ x, P x` or `P ŌåÆ Q`.
-/
TacticDoc intro

/-- `cases x with | constructorŌéü => ... | constructorŌéé => ...`
performs case analysis on `x`, creating one subgoal per constructor.

## Syntax
```
cases x with
| constructorŌéü args => tacticŌéü
| constructorŌéé args => tacticŌéé
```

## When to use it
When you want to consider all possible forms of a value (e.g., natural
numbers are `zero` or `succ n`, propositions with `Ōł©` have `inl` and `inr`).
-/
TacticDoc cases

/-- `have h := expr` introduces a new hypothesis `h` with value `expr`.

## Syntax
```
have h := expr           -- Lean infers the type
have h : type := expr    -- explicit type annotation
have h : type := by ...  -- prove the hypothesis with tactics
```

## When to use it
When you need an intermediate fact that isn't directly in your context.
-/
TacticDoc ┬½have┬╗

/-- `use expr` provides a witness for an existential goal `Ōłā x, P x`.

## Syntax
```
use expr
```

## When to use it
When the goal is `Ōłā x, P x` and you know which `x` works.
After `use expr`, the goal becomes `P expr`.
-/
TacticDoc use

/-- `left` selects the left disjunct of a goal `P Ōł© Q`.

## Syntax
```
left
```

## When to use it
When the goal is `P Ōł© Q` and you want to prove `P`.
-/
TacticDoc left

/-- `right` selects the right disjunct of a goal `P Ōł© Q`.

## Syntax
```
right
```

## When to use it
When the goal is `P Ōł© Q` and you want to prove `Q`.
-/
TacticDoc right

/-- `rfl` closes a goal of the form `a = a` (reflexivity).

## Syntax
```
rfl
```

## When to use it
When both sides of an equality are definitionally equal.
-/
TacticDoc rfl

/-- `constructor` splits a goal with a single constructor (like `Ōł¦` or `Ōåö`)
into its components.

## Syntax
```
constructor
```

## When to use it
When the goal is `P Ōł¦ Q` (splits into `P` and `Q`) or `P Ōåö Q`
(splits into `P ŌåÆ Q` and `Q ŌåÆ P`).
-/
TacticDoc constructor

/-- `apply e` closes or simplifies the goal when `e` is a function,
implication, or theorem whose conclusion matches the goal's type.

## Syntax
```
apply e
```

## When to use it
When the goal is the *conclusion* of some known fact `e`. After
`apply e`, the remaining goals are the *premises* of `e`.

## Example
```
-- h : P ŌåÆ Q, Goal: Q
apply h
-- remaining goal: P
```
-/
TacticDoc apply

/-- `norm_num` evaluates numerical expressions and closes arithmetic
goals that can be decided by computation.

## Syntax
```
norm_num
```

## When to use it
When the goal is a concrete numerical fact like `2 + 3 = 5` or `7 < 10`.

Disabled in levels where manual arithmetic reasoning is the lesson.
-/
TacticDoc norm_num

/-! ### Baseline disabled tactics

These tactics are disabled globally because they close goals that
the player should learn to handle manually.
-/

/-- `trivial` attempts to close the goal using a combination of
basic tactics (`rfl`, `exact True.intro`, `assumption`).

Disabled because it bypasses the learning of specific proof steps.
-/
TacticDoc trivial

/-- `decide` closes goals that are decidable propositions by
evaluating them computationally.

Disabled because it bypasses manual reasoning about finite types.
-/
TacticDoc ┬½decide┬╗

/-- `native_decide` is like `decide` but uses native code evaluation
for faster computation.

Disabled for the same reason as `decide`.
-/
TacticDoc native_decide

/-- `simp` applies a set of simplification lemmas to the goal
and hypotheses.

Disabled in early levels to force manual rewriting and to ensure
the player understands each proof step.
-/
TacticDoc simp

/-- `aesop` is a general-purpose automation tactic that combines
multiple strategies to close goals.

Disabled to force manual proof construction.
-/
TacticDoc aesop

/-- `simp_all` applies `simp` to all hypotheses and the goal
simultaneously.

Disabled for the same reason as `simp`.
-/
TacticDoc simp_all

/-- `by_cases h : P` splits the goal into two cases: one where `h : P`
holds and one where `h : ┬¼P` holds.

Disabled in levels where the taught destructuring + nested `cases`
pattern is the intended approach to case analysis.
-/
TacticDoc by_cases

/-- `tauto` closes goals that are propositional tautologies. It handles
`Ōł©`, `Ōł¦`, `ŌåÆ`, `┬¼`, `Ōåö`, `True`, `False`, and can use `rfl` for equalities.

Disabled in levels where the learner should construct proofs manually
using `left`/`right`, `constructor`, and specific lemmas.
-/
TacticDoc tauto

/-! ### MeetFin-specific disabled tactics and theorems -/

/-- `fin_cases` automatically performs case analysis on elements of
finite types like `Fin n`. It replaces a variable `x : Fin n` with
each possible value `0, 1, ..., n-1`.

Disabled in early levels to force manual case analysis.
-/
TacticDoc fin_cases

/-- `interval_cases` performs case analysis on a natural number or
integer variable whose value is bounded by hypotheses.

Disabled in early levels to force manual case analysis.
-/
TacticDoc interval_cases

/-- `Fin.forall_fin_two` states that a predicate holds for all
elements of `Fin 2` if and only if it holds for `0` and `1`.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.forall_fin_two as "Fin.forall_fin_two" in "Fin"

/-- `Unique.eq_default` states that in a type with exactly one element,
every element equals the default.
-/
TheoremDoc Unique.eq_default as "Unique.eq_default" in "Fin"

/-- `Subsingleton.elim` states that in a type with at most one element,
any two elements are equal.
-/
TheoremDoc Subsingleton.elim as "Subsingleton.elim" in "Fin"

/-! ### FinNavigation-specific disabled theorems -/

/-- `Fin.castSucc_lt_last` states that `i.castSucc < Fin.last n` for
any `i : Fin n`. This follows because `i.castSucc.val = i.val < n = (Fin.last n).val`.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.castSucc_lt_last as "Fin.castSucc_lt_last" in "Fin"

/-- `Fin.castSucc_ne_last` states that `i.castSucc ŌēĀ Fin.last n` for
any `i : Fin n`.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.castSucc_ne_last as "Fin.castSucc_ne_last" in "Fin"

/-- `Fin.castSucc_lt_succ` states that `i.castSucc < i.succ` for
any `i : Fin n`. This follows from the value-level fact
`i.val < i.val + 1`.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.castSucc_lt_succ as "Fin.castSucc_lt_succ" in "Fin"

/-- `Fin.succ_pos` states that `0 < a.succ` for any `a : Fin n`.
This follows from the value-level fact `0 < a.val + 1`.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.succ_pos as "Fin.succ_pos" in "Fin"

/-- `Fin.succ_ne_zero` states that `a.succ ŌēĀ 0` for any `a : Fin n`.
This is the `ŌēĀ` version of `Fin.succ_pos`: since `a.succ.val Ōēź 1 > 0`,
a successor can never equal zero.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.succ_ne_zero as "Fin.succ_ne_zero" in "Fin"

/-- `Fin.succ_inj` states that `a.succ = b.succ Ōåö a = b` for
elements of `Fin n`. In other words, `Fin.succ` is injective.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.succ_inj as "Fin.succ_inj" in "Fin"

/-- `Fin.castSucc_inj` states that `a.castSucc = b.castSucc Ōåö a = b`
for elements of `Fin n`. In other words, `Fin.castSucc` is injective.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.castSucc_inj as "Fin.castSucc_inj" in "Fin"

/-- `Fin.le_last i` states that `i Ōēż Fin.last n` for any `i : Fin (n + 1)`.
Every element is at most the last element.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.le_last as "Fin.le_last" in "Fin"

/-- `Fin.lastCases` is an elimination principle for `Fin (n + 1)`: given
a value for `Fin.last n` and a function on `castSucc` images, produce
a value for any element.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.lastCases as "Fin.lastCases" in "Fin"

/-- `Fin.reverseInduction` is an induction principle for `Fin (n + 1)`
that starts from `Fin.last n` and works downward.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.reverseInduction as "Fin.reverseInduction" in "Fin"

/-- `Fin.cases` is an elimination principle for `Fin (n + 1)`: given
a value for `0` and a function on `succ` images, produce a value for
any element.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.cases as "Fin.cases" in "Fin"

/-- `Fin.eq_zero_or_eq_succ i` states that every `i : Fin (n + 1)`
is either `0` or `j.succ` for some `j : Fin n`.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.eq_zero_or_eq_succ as "Fin.eq_zero_or_eq_succ" in "Fin"

/-! ### FinsetBasics-specific disabled theorems -/

/-- `Finset.mem_insert_self a s` proves `a Ōłł insert a s` directly.

Disabled in levels where the learner should prove membership by
rewriting with `Finset.mem_insert` and choosing `left`/`right`.
-/
TheoremDoc Finset.mem_insert_self as "Finset.mem_insert_self" in "Finset"

/-- `Finset.mem_insert_of_mem h` proves `a Ōłł insert b s` from `h : a Ōłł s`.

Disabled in levels where the learner should prove membership by
rewriting with `Finset.mem_insert` and peeling inserts manually.
-/
TheoremDoc Finset.mem_insert_of_mem as "Finset.mem_insert_of_mem" in "Finset"

/-- `Finset.insert_nonempty a s` proves `(insert a s).Nonempty` directly.

Disabled in levels where the learner should prove Nonempty by
providing a witness with `use` and proving membership.
-/
TheoremDoc Finset.insert_nonempty as "Finset.insert_nonempty" in "Finset"

/-- `Finset.singleton_nonempty a` proves `({a}).Nonempty` directly.

Disabled in levels where the learner should prove Nonempty by
providing a witness with `use` and proving membership.
-/
TheoremDoc Finset.singleton_nonempty as "Finset.singleton_nonempty" in "Finset"

/-- `Finset.not_mem_range_self` proves `n Ōłē Finset.range n` directly.

Disabled in levels where the learner should prove non-membership
by assuming membership, rewriting with `mem_range`, and deriving
a contradiction with `omega`.
-/
TheoremDoc Finset.not_mem_range_self as "Finset.not_mem_range_self" in "Finset"

/-! ### FinTuples-specific definitions and theorems -/

/-- Non-dependent version of `Fin.cons_zero`.
`Fin.cons a f 0 = a` ŌĆö accessing the head of a cons'd tuple returns the prepended element. -/
theorem Fin.cons_val_zero {n : ŌäĢ} {╬▒ : Type*} (a : ╬▒) (f : Fin n ŌåÆ ╬▒) :
    (Fin.cons a f : Fin (n+1) ŌåÆ ╬▒) 0 = a := rfl

/-- Non-dependent version of `Fin.cons_succ`.
`Fin.cons a f i.succ = f i` ŌĆö accessing later elements of a cons'd tuple returns the tail. -/
theorem Fin.cons_val_succ {n : ŌäĢ} {╬▒ : Type*} (a : ╬▒) (f : Fin n ŌåÆ ╬▒) (i : Fin n) :
    (Fin.cons a f : Fin (n+1) ŌåÆ ╬▒) i.succ = f i := rfl

/-- `vecSnoc f x` appends element `x` to the end of tuple `f`,
producing a tuple of length `n + 1`.

This is the non-dependent wrapper around `Fin.snoc` for use with
concrete types like `Fin n ŌåÆ ŌäĢ`.

## Type
`vecSnoc : (Fin n ŌåÆ ╬▒) ŌåÆ ╬▒ ŌåÆ Fin (n + 1) ŌåÆ ╬▒`

## Element access
- `vecSnoc f x (Fin.last n) = x` (the appended element)
- `vecSnoc f x i.castSucc = f i` (the original tuple)

## Example
`vecSnoc ![10, 20] 30 = ![10, 20, 30]`
-/
def vecSnoc {n : ŌäĢ} {╬▒ : Type*} (f : Fin n ŌåÆ ╬▒) (x : ╬▒) : Fin (n + 1) ŌåÆ ╬▒ :=
  @Fin.snoc n (fun _ => ╬▒) f x

/-- Accessing the last element of a snoc'd tuple gives back the appended element. -/
theorem vecSnoc_last {n : ŌäĢ} {╬▒ : Type*} (f : Fin n ŌåÆ ╬▒) (x : ╬▒) :
    vecSnoc f x (Fin.last n) = x :=
  @Fin.snoc_last n (fun _ => ╬▒) x f

/-- Accessing an earlier element of a snoc'd tuple gives the original tuple's value. -/
theorem vecSnoc_castSucc {n : ŌäĢ} {╬▒ : Type*} (f : Fin n ŌåÆ ╬▒) (x : ╬▒) (i : Fin n) :
    vecSnoc f x i.castSucc = f i :=
  @Fin.snoc_castSucc n (fun _ => ╬▒) x f i

/-- Appending the last element to the init recovers the original tuple. -/
theorem vecSnoc_self_init {n : ŌäĢ} {╬▒ : Type*} (f : Fin (n + 1) ŌåÆ ╬▒) :
    vecSnoc (Fin.init f) (f (Fin.last n)) = f :=
  Fin.snoc_init_self f

/-- `vecSnoc_self_init f` states that
`vecSnoc (Fin.init f) (f (Fin.last n)) = f`.

Every tuple equals its init snoc'd with its last element.

## Syntax
```
exact vecSnoc_self_init f  -- proves vecSnoc (init f) (f (last n)) = f
```

## When to use it
When you need to decompose a tuple into its earlier elements
and its last element.
-/
TheoremDoc vecSnoc_self_init as "vecSnoc_self_init" in "Fin"

/-- `funext` is the function extensionality theorem: if two functions
agree on every input, they are equal.

Disabled in levels where the `ext` tactic should be used interactively.
-/
TheoremDoc funext as "funext" in "Fin"

/-- `congrFun h a` extracts pointwise agreement from a function equality.
If `h : f = g`, then `congrFun h a : f a = g a`.

## Syntax
```
have h0 := congrFun h 0     -- extract agreement at index 0
have hi := congrFun h i.succ -- extract agreement at index i.succ
```

## When to use it
When you have `h : f = g` and need to deduce that `f a = g a` at a
specific input `a`. This is the **pointwise extraction** move: turn
a global function equality into a local value equality.
-/
TheoremDoc congrFun as "congrFun" in "Fin"

/-- `Function.comp_apply` states that `(g Ōłś f) x = g (f x)`.

## Syntax
```
rw [Function.comp_apply]  -- unfolds (g Ōłś f) x to g (f x)
```

## When to use it
When you need to unfold a composition `(g Ōłś f) x` to the explicit
application `g (f x)` ŌĆö for example, before substituting formulas
for `f` and `g` with `rw`.
-/
TheoremDoc Function.comp_apply as "Function.comp_apply" in "Fin"

For E7 specifically, constructing the bijection between Fin 2 and Bool is the core challenge ŌĆö I'd need to define both directions and prove they're inverses, which is more involved than the other enrichment items. ŌåÆ Fin 2 and prove they're inverses. This requires Bool case analysis, which hasn't been taught. And Equiv hasn't been introduced. This might actually be pedagogically inappropriate for MeetFin ŌĆö it introduces too many new concepts simultaneously. It could work in PsetFin since it's an enrichment world, but it would need Bool patterns...

Actually, thinking more carefully: the only valid rejection reason is that it would be pedagogically WORSE. Would adding a Fin 2 Ōēā Bool level make things worse? I think it would be a slight stretch since Bool hasn't been taught, but we could frame it as a fun application. However, the level would require `match` on Bool or `cases` on Bool, which is a new skill. And proving the compositions are identity would require function extensionality...

The learner has `ext` for function extensionality from FinTuples and they have `cases`, so they could define the forward and backward functions using pattern matching. But implementing this with conditionals would require `decide`, which is disabled. For a PsetFin level, I could frame it as an existence statement where they prove the two functions are mutual inverses without needing to construct them explicitly. The real question is whether this level would actually help or just add unnecessary complexity.

The enrichment review recommends it, and the gate decision puts it in a specific category. Let me check what that category actually says.

Looking at the gate decision more carefully, E7 is marked "Consider" ŌĆö not a firm yes, but it's still in the implementation list. Given that the enrichment review explicitly recommends it as something the course mentions but never delivers, I should implement it as a proper level rather than just adding text.

So my plan is to tackle all the P1 fixes, the low-effort enrichment changes, E9 which got a clear yes, and then E7, E8, and E13 as the new levels ŌĆö with E7 being the priority since it has the strongest recommendation. it as a new level. For E8 and E13, I'll consider them.

E8 is about the reverse direction of the Finset.mem_range equivalence ŌĆö showing that membership implies the bound. It's trivial on its own, but making the iff explicit has pedagogical value, so I'll add it as a level to reinforce the equivalence and teach the `rw [...] at h` pattern.

E13 generalizes the concrete example from L05 to an arbitrary n, showing that n itself isn't in range n. This parallels the progression in MeetFin and is worth including as a level.

Now I'm mapping out where these fit in FinsetBasics. The current structure has 13 levels, and I need to insert E9 (empty range), E8 (reverse direction), and E13 (general n Ōłē range n) at strategic points to build naturally from concrete to abstract.

import Game.Levels.FinsetBasics.L01_WhatIsAFinset
import Game.Levels.FinsetBasics.L02_FindingDeeperElements
import Game.Levels.FinsetBasics.L03_TheEmptyFinset
import Game.Levels.FinsetBasics.L04_RangeOfNaturals
import Game.Levels.FinsetBasics.L05_TheRangeBoundary
import Game.Levels.FinsetBasics.L06_VariableRange
import Game.Levels.FinsetBasics.L07_ProvingNonMembership
import Game.Levels.FinsetBasics.L08_ReadingMembership
import Game.Levels.FinsetBasics.L09_InsertIsIdempotent
import Game.Levels.FinsetBasics.L10_NonemptyFinsets

import Game.Levels.FinsetBasics.L12_DecideShortcut
import Game.Levels.FinsetBasics.L13_Boss

World "FinsetBasics"
Title "Finset Basics"

Introduction "
# Finset Basics: Finite Sets

In Phase 1, you learned about `Fin n` ŌĆö a finite *index type* where
each element is a number with a bound proof. Functions `Fin n ŌåÆ ╬▒`
gave you *ordered tuples*: data accessed by position.

But not everything in mathematics has a natural ordering. The set of
prime divisors of 30 is $\\{2, 3, 5\\}$ ŌĆö there's no reason 2 should
come 'first'. The elements of a group satisfying some property form a
*collection defined by membership*, not by position.

This is where `Finset` comes in. A `Finset ╬▒` is an **unordered finite
collection** of elements from `╬▒`, with no duplicates and decidable
membership. Think of it as a mathematical finite set, formalized.

The key operations:
- **Literal notation**: `\\{1, 2, 3\\}` builds a concrete finset
- **`Ōłģ`**: the empty finset
- **`insert a s`**: add an element (no-op if already present)
- **`Finset.range n`**: the set $\\{0, 1, \\ldots, n-1\\}$

The key proof move: to show `x Ōłł s`, unfold the set structure using
*membership lemmas* (`Finset.mem_insert`, `Finset.mem_singleton`,
`Finset.mem_range`) until the goal reduces to something `rfl` or
`omega` can close.

The bridge from Phase 1: `Finset.range n` contains exactly the same
elements as `Fin n` ŌĆö the numbers $0$ through $n-1$. Where `Fin n`
gave you *indexed access* (`f i` for `i : Fin n`), `Finset.range n`
gives you *membership* (`k Ōłł Finset.range n Ōåö k < n`). Same elements,
different interface.

In this world you'll learn to prove membership, non-membership,
and basic structural facts about finsets. The boss at the end
combines all these moves.
"

import Game.Metadata

World "FinsetBasics"
Level 4

Title "Range of Natural Numbers"

Introduction "
# Finset.range

`Finset.range n` is the finset $\\{0, 1, 2, \\ldots, n-1\\}$ ŌĆö the
first `n` natural numbers.

This connects directly to `Fin n` from Phase 1: the elements of
`Finset.range n` are exactly the values that `Fin n` elements can
take. Where `Fin n` gave you typed indices, `Finset.range n` gives
you a set you can reason about with membership.

The membership lemma is `Finset.mem_range`:
$$m \\in \\texttt{Finset.range } n \\;\\longleftrightarrow\\; m < n$$

**Warning**: `Finset.range n` contains $\\{0, \\ldots, n-1\\}$, **not**
$\\{0, \\ldots, n\\}$. The boundary `n` itself is *not* in the range.
This matches `Fin n` (whose elements satisfy `k < n`).

**Your task**: Prove that `3 Ōłł Finset.range 5`.

Use `rw [Finset.mem_range]` to convert to `3 < 5`, then `omega`.
"

/-- 3 is in the range {0, 1, 2, 3, 4}. -/
Statement : (3 : ŌäĢ) Ōłł Finset.range 5 := by
  Hint "Use `rw [Finset.mem_range]` to convert membership into an inequality."
  rw [Finset.mem_range]
  Hint "The goal is `3 < 5`. Close it with `omega`."
  omega

Conclusion "
The `rw [Finset.mem_range]; omega` pattern is extremely useful.
It reduces finset membership to ordinary arithmetic ŌĆö territory
you know well from `Fin` proofs.

`Finset.range` appears throughout finite mathematics:
- Summing over `{0, ..., n-1}`: `Ōłæ i Ōłł Finset.range n, f i`
- Counting elements satisfying a property in a range
- Connecting `Fin n` indices to set-level reasoning

**Boundary case**: `Finset.range 0 = Ōłģ` ŌĆö the range with zero
elements is empty. This parallels `Fin 0` being the empty type
from Phase 1. Where `Fin 0` had no inhabitants (no `i : Fin 0`
exists), `Finset.range 0` has no members (no `k Ōłł Finset.range 0`).
Same emptiness, different interface.

Remember: `range n` excludes `n` itself. The next level makes
this explicit.
"

/-- `Finset.range n` is the finset `{0, 1, 2, ..., n-1}`.

## Type
`Finset.range : ŌäĢ ŌåÆ Finset ŌäĢ`

## Elements
`Finset.range n` contains exactly the natural numbers `0` through `n - 1`.

## Warning
`Finset.range n` does NOT include `n`. It has `n` elements:
`{0, 1, ..., n-1}`.

## Example
`Finset.range 3 = {0, 1, 2}` (not `{0, 1, 2, 3}`)
-/
DefinitionDoc Finset.range as "Finset.range"

/-- `Finset.mem_range` states that `m Ōłł Finset.range n Ōåö m < n`.

## Syntax
```
rw [Finset.mem_range]       -- in the goal
rw [Finset.mem_range] at h  -- in a hypothesis
```

## When to use it
When the goal or a hypothesis involves `x Ōłł Finset.range n`.
After rewriting, the goal becomes an inequality that `omega` can
typically close.

## Example
```
-- Goal: 3 Ōłł Finset.range 5
rw [Finset.mem_range]
-- Goal: 3 < 5
omega
```
-/
TheoremDoc Finset.mem_range as "Finset.mem_range" in "Finset"

NewDefinition Finset.range
NewTheorem Finset.mem_range

import Game.Metadata

World "FinsetBasics"
Level 5

Title "The Range Boundary"

Introduction "
# The Range Boundary

`Finset.range 5 = {0, 1, 2, 3, 4}`. Note that `5` itself is **not**
in the range. This is a common source of off-by-one errors.

To prove `5 Ōłē Finset.range 5`, you need to prove a **non-membership**
statement. The `Ōłē` symbol means `┬¼ Ōłł` ŌĆö i.e., the membership is false.

The proof pattern for non-membership:
1. `intro h` ŌĆö assume `h : 5 Ōłł Finset.range 5` (proof by contradiction)
2. `rw [Finset.mem_range] at h` ŌĆö convert to `h : 5 < 5`
3. `omega` ŌĆö contradiction (`5 < 5` is false)

This is the first time you'll rewrite **a hypothesis** rather than
the goal. The `at h` modifier tells `rw` to rewrite inside `h`.
You practiced this in the Fin worlds ŌĆö the same `at` modifier works
for any rewrite.
"

/-- 5 is NOT in the range {0, 1, 2, 3, 4}. -/
Statement : (5 : ŌäĢ) Ōłē Finset.range 5 := by
  Hint "The goal is `5 Ōłē Finset.range 5`, which means
  `┬¼ (5 Ōłł Finset.range 5)`. Use `intro h` to assume
  `h : 5 Ōłł Finset.range 5` and aim for a contradiction."
  intro h
  Hint "Now you have `{h}` and the goal is `False`.
  Rewrite `{h}` using `rw [Finset.mem_range] at h` to get
  `h : 5 < 5`."
  rw [Finset.mem_range] at h
  Hint "Now `{h}` is contradictory. `omega` sees this
  and closes the goal."
  omega

Conclusion "
The non-membership pattern:
1. `intro h` ŌĆö assume membership
2. `rw [... ] at h` ŌĆö unfold to something concrete
3. `omega` ŌĆö derive a contradiction

This is **proof by contradiction** in a very targeted form: you don't
need to invoke any fancy contradiction tactic ŌĆö just show that the
hypothesis leads to a false arithmetic statement.

This pattern extends beyond `range`. In the next level, you'll use
the same technique to prove non-membership in a literal finset
like `{1, 2, 3}`.
"

1ŌåÆimport Game.Metadata
     2ŌåÆ
     3ŌåÆWorld "FinsetBasics"
     4ŌåÆLevel 6
     5ŌåÆ
     6ŌåÆTitle "Variable Range Membership"
     7ŌåÆ
     8ŌåÆIntroduction "
     9ŌåÆ# The Bridge from Fin to Finset
    10ŌåÆ
    11ŌåÆIn Level 4, you proved `3 Ōłł Finset.range 5` using concrete numbers.
    12ŌåÆIn Level 5, you proved `5 Ōłē Finset.range 5` at the boundary.
    13ŌåÆ
    14ŌåÆBut the real power of `Finset.mem_range` is with **variables**.
    15ŌåÆThe introduction to this world promised a bridge between `Fin n`
    16ŌåÆ(Phase 1) and `Finset.range n` (Phase 2). Here it is.
    17ŌåÆ
    18ŌåÆGiven a hypothesis `h : i < n`, prove that `i Ōłł Finset.range n`.

import Game.Metadata

World "FinsetBasics"
Level 6

Title "Variable Range Membership"

Introduction "
# The Bridge from Fin to Finset

In Level 4, you proved `3 Ōłł Finset.range 5` using concrete numbers.
In Level 5, you proved `5 Ōłē Finset.range 5` at the boundary.

But the real power of `Finset.mem_range` is with **variables**.
The introduction to this world promised a bridge between `Fin n`
(Phase 1) and `Finset.range n` (Phase 2). Here it is.

Given a hypothesis `h : i < n`, prove that `i Ōłł Finset.range n`.
The proof is the same pattern as Level 4:

1. `rw [Finset.mem_range]` ŌĆö converts the goal to `i < n`
2. `exact h` ŌĆö the hypothesis is exactly what we need

This is the **variable-n** version of the Phase 1 bound proof.
In Phase 1, you proved `3 < 5` to construct `Ō¤©3, by omegaŌ¤® : Fin 5`.
Here, you use the same bound `i < n` to prove membership in
`Finset.range n`. Same arithmetic, different packaging.
"

/-- An element below n belongs to Finset.range n. -/
Statement (n i : ŌäĢ) (h : i < n) : i Ōłł Finset.range n := by
  Hint "Use `rw [Finset.mem_range]` to convert the membership goal
  to an inequality."
  rw [Finset.mem_range]
  Hint "The goal is `i < n`, which is exactly `{h}`."
  exact h

Conclusion "
This is the bridge the introduction promised: `Finset.range n`
contains exactly the numbers that could be values of `Fin n`
elements. The proof move `rw [Finset.mem_range]; exact h` is the
finset analog of the `Ō¤©i, hŌ¤® : Fin n` constructor.

| Phase 1 (Fin) | Phase 2 (Finset) |
|---|---|
| `Ō¤©i, hŌ¤® : Fin n` | `i Ōłł Finset.range n` |
| Requires `h : i < n` | Requires `h : i < n` |
| Typed index access | Set membership |

The bound proof `h : i < n` is the common currency between the
two interfaces. This connection becomes load-bearing later: when
you sum over `Finset.range n`, the bound proofs from `Fin n` will
transfer directly.
"

import Game.Metadata

World "MeetFin"
Level 1

Title "What is Fin n?"

Introduction "
# What is `Fin n`?

In mathematics, we often work with finite index sets like
$\\{0, 1, 2, 3, 4\\}$ ŌĆö the first $n$ natural numbers.

In Lean 4, this is captured by the type `Fin n`. An element of `Fin n`
is a natural number `k` **together with a proof** that `k < n`.

More precisely:
$$\\texttt{Fin } n = \\{ k : \\mathbb{N} \\mid k < n \\}$$

**Why not just use `ŌäĢ`?** If you index a length-5 vector with a plain
natural number, nothing stops you from using `7` ŌĆö the type checker
can't tell it's out of bounds. With `Fin 5`, the bound is part of the
type: you literally *cannot construct* an element unless you prove it's
in range. The type system enforces the constraint for you.

This is an example of a *subtype* ŌĆö a type carved out of a larger type
by a predicate. `Fin n` is the subtype `{ k : ŌäĢ // k < n }`.

To construct an element of `Fin n`, use the **anonymous constructor**
`Ō¤©k, proofŌ¤®`, where:
- `k` is the natural number
- `proof` shows `k < n`

For the proof part, the `omega` tactic handles routine arithmetic
goals like `3 < 5`.

**Your task**: Construct any element of `Fin 5`. There are five to
choose from: $0, 1, 2, 3, 4$.

Try `exact Ō¤©3, by omegaŌ¤®`.

*Notation*: type `Ō¤©` as `\\langle` and `Ō¤®` as `\\rangle`.
"

-- Design decision: The goal type `Fin 5` is intentionally unconstrained.
-- `exact 0`, `exact 3`, etc. all work. This is accepted as onboarding
-- gentleness: the level's purpose is to introduce the Ō¤©k, by omegaŌ¤®
-- constructor pattern, not to force it. The conclusion explains that
-- the explicit constructor becomes mandatory when the bound is a variable
-- (Level 12+). First-level accessibility outweighs forcing here.

/-- Constructs a specific element of `Fin 5`. -/
Statement : Fin 5 := by
  Hint "Use the anonymous constructor `Ō¤©k, by omegaŌ¤®` where `k` is any
  natural number less than 5. For example, `exact Ō¤©3, by omegaŌ¤®`."
  exact Ō¤©3, by omegaŌ¤®

Conclusion "
Well done! You just constructed the element $3$ as a term of type
`Fin 5`.

The key pattern: to build a `Fin n`, you provide **two things** ŌĆö a
number and a proof that it's in range. The `Ō¤©k, by omegaŌ¤®` idiom
will appear throughout this world.

In ordinary mathematics, saying *let $x = 3 \\in \\{0, \\ldots, 4\\}$*
is informal. In Lean, you must **prove** the element is in range.
That's what the second component does.

**Design note**: `exact 3` also works here ŌĆö Lean can automatically
coerce a numeric literal into `Fin 5` when the bound is known.
We intentionally allow this shortcut in this first level so you can
focus on understanding the *structure* of `Fin` without getting stuck
on syntax. But practice the `Ō¤©k, by omegaŌ¤®` pattern now, because
starting in Level 12, the type bound will be a variable `n` ŌĆö not a
concrete number ŌĆö and the automatic coercion won't work. The explicit
constructor is the general-purpose tool.
"

/-- `omega` closes goals involving linear arithmetic over `ŌäĢ` and `Ōäż`.
It handles equalities (`=`), inequalities (`<`, `Ōēż`, `Ōēź`, `>`),
and negated equalities (`ŌēĀ`).

## Syntax
```
omega
```

## When to use it
- When the goal follows from linear arithmetic: `3 < 5`, `n + 1 Ōēż m`
- When hypotheses contradict each other: if you have `h1 : x = 5` and
  `h2 : x < 5`, omega sees the contradiction and closes any goal
- When multiple hypotheses combine: omega reasons across ALL hypotheses
  simultaneously, not just one at a time

## Examples
```
-- Goal: 3 < 5
omega

-- h1 : x = 5, h2 : x < 5 ŌåÆ closes any goal (contradiction)
omega

-- h1 : a Ōēż b, h2 : b < c ŌåÆ Goal: a < c
omega
```

## Warning
`omega` only handles *linear* arithmetic. It cannot solve goals
involving multiplication of variables (like `n * m < n * n + 1`).
-/
TacticDoc omega

/-- `Fin n` is the type of natural numbers strictly less than `n`.

An element of `Fin n` consists of:
- `val : ŌäĢ` ŌĆö the underlying natural number
- `isLt : val < n` ŌĆö a proof that the number is in range

`Fin n` is the subtype `{ k : ŌäĢ // k < n }`.

## Construction
Use the anonymous constructor: `Ō¤©k, proofŌ¤®`

## Example
`Ō¤©3, by omegaŌ¤® : Fin 5` ŌĆö the number 3 with a proof that `3 < 5`.

## Warning
`Fin n` has elements `0, 1, ..., n-1` (NOT `1, ..., n`).
`Fin 0` is **empty** ŌĆö there is no natural number less than 0.
-/
DefinitionDoc Fin as "Fin"

/-- `Fin.mk val isLt` constructs an element of `Fin n` from a natural
number `val` and a proof `isLt : val < n`.

Equivalently, use the anonymous constructor `Ō¤©val, isLtŌ¤®`.

## Example
`Ō¤©3, by omegaŌ¤® : Fin 5`
-/
DefinitionDoc Fin.mk as "Fin.mk"

NewTactic omega exact rw intro cases ┬½have┬╗ use left right rfl constructor apply
NewDefinition Fin Fin.mk

import Game.Metadata

World "MeetFin"
Level 4

Title "Combining Bounds"

Introduction "
# Two Elements, Two Bounds

In Level 3, you extracted a single bound with `.isLt` and used `omega`.
Here you'll combine bounds from **two** elements.

Every `Fin 5` element has value less than 5. So if `i` and `j` are
both in `Fin 5`, their values satisfy `i.val < 5` and `j.val < 5`.
What can you conclude about their sum?

Since `i.val Ōēż 4` and `j.val Ōēż 4`, the sum `i.val + j.val Ōēż 8`.

**Strategy**: Extract both bound proofs with `have`, then let `omega`
chain them together.

Step by step:
1. `have h1 := i.isLt` ŌĆö gives `h1 : i.val < 5`
2. `have h2 := j.isLt` ŌĆö gives `h2 : j.val < 5`
3. `omega` ŌĆö deduces `i.val + j.val Ōēż 8` from both bounds
"

/-- The sum of two Fin 5 values is at most 8. -/
Statement (i j : Fin 5) : i.val + j.val Ōēż 8 := by
  Hint "Extract the first bound: `have h1 := i.isLt`."
  have h1 := i.isLt
  Hint "Extract the second bound: `have h2 := j.isLt`."
  have h2 := j.isLt
  Hint "Now `{h1}` says `i.val < 5` and `{h2}` says `j.val < 5`.
  `omega` combines these to prove `i.val + j.val Ōēż 8`."
  omega

Conclusion "
You combined two `.isLt` bounds and let `omega` chain them.

This demonstrates a key principle: **every `Fin n` element carries
useful information** in its bound proof. When you have multiple
`Fin` elements, their bounds combine to constrain their values.

The pattern generalizes:
- One element: `have h := i.isLt; omega`
- Two elements: extract both bounds, then `omega`
- $k$ elements: extract all bounds, then `omega`

`omega` reasons across ALL hypotheses simultaneously ŌĆö you just need
to make the bounds visible by extracting them with `have`.

The `Ō¤©value, proofŌ¤®` constructor (Level 1) and the `.val` / `.isLt`
extractors (Levels 2ŌĆō3) form a complete round-trip: you can take a
`Fin` element apart and put it back together. You'll see this
round-trip formalized starting in Level 5.
"

import Game.Metadata

World "MeetFin"
Level 6

Title "The Converse Direction"

Introduction "
# Fin Equality Implies Value Equality

In Level 5, you proved that equal values imply equal `Fin` elements
(the backward direction of `Fin.ext_iff`).

The converse is also true: if two `Fin` elements are equal, then
their values are equal. This follows from a basic principle: applying
a function to both sides of an equality preserves the equality. Since
`.val` is a function from `Fin n` to `ŌäĢ`, if `i = j` then
`i.val = j.val`.

In Lean, the simplest proof uses `rw` directly. If `h : i = j`, then
`rw [h]` replaces `i` with `j` in the goal, turning `i.val = j.val`
into `j.val = j.val` ŌĆö which closes by reflexivity.

**Your task**: Prove that `Fin` equality implies value equality.
"

/-- If two `Fin` elements are equal, their values are equal. -/
Statement (i j : Fin 5) (h : i = j) : i.val = j.val := by
  Hint "You have `{h}` saying `i = j`. Use `rw [h]` to replace `i`
  with `j` in the goal."
  Hint (hidden := true) "`rw [h]` turns the goal into `j.val = j.val`,
  which Lean closes automatically."
  rw [h]

Conclusion "
Both directions of `Fin.ext_iff` are now concrete:

| Direction | Statement | Proof |
|---|---|---|
| Values ŌåÆ Elements | `i.val = j.val ŌåÆ i = j` | `rw [Fin.ext_iff]; exact h` |
| Elements ŌåÆ Values | `i = j ŌåÆ i.val = j.val` | `rw [h]` |

Together, they say: **`Fin` equality is completely determined by
value equality.** The type-level wrapper carries no extra
information beyond the natural number inside.

**The congruence principle**: The proof pattern here ŌĆö if `h : i = j`
then `rw [h]` turns `i.val` into `j.val` ŌĆö is an instance of a
general fact: *applying any function to both sides of an equality
preserves the equality*. If `h : a = b` and `f` is any function,
then `f a = f b`. In Lean, `rw [h]` handles this automatically.
This principle (called `congr_arg` in Lean's library) will appear
whenever you transfer an equality through a function.

In the next level, you'll learn to rewrite hypotheses (not just the
goal) using `rw [...] at h`.
"

import Game.Metadata

World "MeetFin"
Level 2

Title "The Number Inside"

Introduction "
# Extracting the Natural Number

Every element `i : Fin n` carries two pieces of data:
- `i.val : ŌäĢ` ŌĆö the underlying natural number
- `i.isLt : i.val < n` ŌĆö the proof that it's in range

The field `.val` lets you **extract** the natural number from a `Fin`
element. The field `.isLt` gives you the **bound proof** directly.

Lean also has a *coercion* notation: when a natural number is expected,
you can write `(i : ŌäĢ)` or `Ōåæi` (typed `\\uparrow i`), and Lean
automatically inserts `.val`. So `(i : ŌäĢ)`, `Ōåæi`, and `i.val` all
mean the same thing.

Look at the goal: it says `(Ōåæi : ŌäĢ) = 3`. The hypothesis `h` says
`i.val = 3`. Since `Ōåæi` IS `i.val`, the hypothesis `h` matches the
goal exactly.

**Your task**: Use `exact h` to close the goal.
"

/-- The coercion `Ōåæi` is the same as `i.val`. -/
Statement (i : Fin 5) (h : i.val = 3) : (i : ŌäĢ) = 3 := by
  Hint "The goal `(Ōåæi : ŌäĢ) = 3` is the same as `i.val = 3`. The
  hypothesis `{h}` says exactly `i.val = 3`. Try `exact h`."
  Hint (hidden := true) "`exact h` works because `(i : ŌäĢ)` and `i.val`
  are definitionally the same ŌĆö Lean sees no difference."
  exact h

Conclusion "
You've just seen that `Ōåæi`, `(i : ŌäĢ)`, and `i.val` are all the same
thing ŌĆö three notations for extracting the natural number from a `Fin`
element.

| Notation | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `i.val` | Dot notation for the value field |
| `Ōåæi` | Coercion arrow (typed `\\uparrow`) |
| `(i : ŌäĢ)` | Type ascription coercion |

All three are **definitionally equal** in Lean ŌĆö the type checker
treats them as identical without any proof needed.

The other field, `i.isLt`, gives you a proof that `i.val < n`. You'll
use `.isLt` in the next level to prove a bound.
"

/-- `Fin.val` extracts the underlying natural number from a `Fin n`
element. If `i : Fin n`, then `i.val : ŌäĢ` satisfies `i.val < n`.

The coercion `Ōåæi` (typed `\\uparrow i`) is notation for `i.val`
when a natural number is expected.

## Example
If `i : Fin 5` and `i.val = 3`, then:
- `i.val = 3`
- `(Ōåæi : ŌäĢ) = 3`

## Related
`i.isLt` gives a proof that `i.val < n`.
-/
DefinitionDoc Fin.val as "Fin.val"

NewDefinition Fin.val

DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases

     8ŌåÆIntroduction "
     9ŌåÆ# Fin 5 Does Not Contain 5
    10ŌåÆ

    12ŌåÆ$\\{0, 1, \\ldots, n-1\\}$ with $\\{0, 1, \\ldots, n\\}$. The elements

    14ŌåÆ

    16ŌåÆ

    18ŌåÆcontradiction. Then use `x.isLt` (which says `x.val < 5`) to get a
    19ŌåÆcontradiction with `h` via `omega`.
    20ŌåÆ
    21ŌåÆStep by step:
    22ŌåÆ1. `intro x` ŌĆö introduces the element
    23ŌåÆ2. `intro h` ŌĆö assumes `x.val = 5` (since `ŌēĀ` means `= ŌåÆ False`)
    24ŌåÆ3. `have hlt := x.isLt` ŌĆö extracts the bound `x.val < 5`

    26ŌåÆ"
    27ŌåÆ

    29ŌåÆStatement : ŌłĆ x : Fin 5, x.val ŌēĀ 5 := by
    30ŌåÆ  Hint "Start with `intro x` to introduce the element, then `intro h`
    31ŌåÆ  to assume `x.val = 5` for contradiction."
    32ŌåÆ  intro x
    33ŌåÆ  intro h
    34ŌåÆ  Hint "Now extract the bound proof: `have hlt := x.isLt`. This gives
    35ŌåÆ  `hlt : x.val < 5`."
    36ŌåÆ  have hlt := x.isLt
    37ŌåÆ  Hint "You have `{h}` saying `x.val = 5` and `{hlt}` saying
    38ŌåÆ  `x.val < 5`. These contradict each other ŌĆö `omega` sees this."
    39ŌåÆ  omega
    40ŌåÆ
    41ŌåÆConclusion "
    42ŌåÆYou've now proved what the introduction warned about: `Fin 5` contains
    43ŌåÆ$\\{0, 1, 2, 3, 4\\}$ and **not** 5.
    44ŌåÆ
    45ŌåÆThe key ingredient was `.isLt`: every `x : Fin n` carries a proof
    46ŌåÆthat `x.val < n`. Combined with the assumption `x.val = n`, you get
    47ŌåÆa contradiction that `omega` resolves.
    48ŌåÆ
    49ŌåÆNotice that `omega` is doing something new here: in Level 1, it proved
    50ŌåÆa simple fact (`3 < 5`). Here, it **derives a contradiction** from two
    51ŌåÆhypotheses (`x.val = 5` and `x.val < 5`). `omega` reasons across all
    52ŌåÆhypotheses simultaneously ŌĆö it sees everything in your context that
    53ŌåÆinvolves linear arithmetic over `ŌäĢ` and `Ōäż`, and either proves the
    54ŌåÆgoal or finds a contradiction. This makes it your go-to tactic for
    55ŌåÆany arithmetic reasoning in this course.
    56ŌåÆ
    57ŌåÆThis pattern ŌĆö *extract the bound, derive a contradiction* ŌĆö appears
    58ŌåÆwhenever someone (or some proof branch) tries to use an out-of-range

    60ŌåÆprovides.
    61ŌåÆ

    63ŌåÆ$\\{0,1,2,3,4\\}$ instead of $\\{1,2,3,4,5\\}$. The reason becomes
    64ŌåÆclear in the next world: the embedding `castSucc : Fin n -> Fin (n+1)`
    65ŌåÆpreserves values exactly (`i.castSucc.val = i.val`), and `succ`
    66ŌåÆincrements by exactly 1 (`i.succ.val = i.val + 1`). With 1-indexing,
    67ŌåÆthese clean relationships would need offsets everywhere, making

    69ŌåÆ"

file.content:
import Game.Metadata

World "MeetFin"
Level 3

Title "The Upper Bound"

Introduction "
# Fin 5 Does Not Contain 5

The most common misconception about `Fin n` is confusing
$\\{0, 1, \\ldots, n-1\\}$ with $\\{0, 1, \\ldots, n\\}$. The elements
of `Fin 5` are $\\{0, 1, 2, 3, 4\\}$ ŌĆö **not** $\\{0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5\\}$.

Let's make this concrete: no element of `Fin 5` has value `5`.

**Strategy**: Introduce `x` and assume `h : x.val = 5` for
contradiction. Then use `x.isLt` (which says `x.val < 5`) to get a
contradiction with `h` via `omega`.

Step by step:
1. `intro x` ŌĆö introduces the element
2. `intro h` ŌĆö assumes `x.val = 5` (since `ŌēĀ` means `= ŌåÆ False`)
3. `have hlt := x.isLt` ŌĆö extracts the bound `x.val < 5`
4. `omega` ŌĆö sees `x.val = 5` and `x.val < 5` are contradictory
"

/-- No element of `Fin 5` has value `5`. -/
Statement : ŌłĆ x : Fin 5, x.val ŌēĀ 5 := by
  Hint "Start with `intro x` to introduce the element, then `intro h`
  to assume `x.val = 5` for contradiction."
  intro x
  intro h
  Hint "Now extract the bound proof: `have hlt := x.isLt`. This gives
  `hlt : x.val < 5`."
  have hlt := x.isLt
  Hint "You have `{h}` saying `x.val = 5` and `{hlt}` saying
  `x.val < 5`. These contradict each other ŌĆö `omega` sees this."
  omega

Conclusion "
You've now proved what the introduction warned about: `Fin 5` contains
$\\{0, 1, 2, 3, 4\\}$ and **not** 5.

The key ingredient was `.isLt`: every `x : Fin n` carries a proof
that `x.val < n`. Combined with the assumption `x.val = n`, you get
a contradiction that `omega` resolves.

Notice that `omega` is doing something new here: in Level 1, it proved
a simple fact (`3 < 5`). Here, it **derives a contradiction** from two
hypotheses (`x.val = 5` and `x.val < 5`). `omega` reasons across all
hypotheses simultaneously ŌĆö it sees everything in your context that
involves linear arithmetic over `ŌäĢ` and `Ōäż`, and either proves the
goal or finds a contradiction. This makes it your go-to tactic for
any arithmetic reasoning in this course.

This pattern ŌĆö *extract the bound, derive a contradiction* ŌĆö appears
whenever someone (or some proof branch) tries to use an out-of-range
value. The bound proof `.isLt` is the safety net that the type system
provides.

**Why does Fin start at 0?** You might wonder why `Fin 5` is
$\\{0,1,2,3,4\\}$ instead of $\\{1,2,3,4,5\\}$. The reason becomes
clear in the next world: the embedding `castSucc : Fin n -> Fin (n+1)`
preserves values exactly (`i.castSucc.val = i.val`), and `succ`
increments by exactly 1 (`i.succ.val = i.val + 1`). With 1-indexing,
these clean relationships would need offsets everywhere, making
every proof longer and harder to read.
"

DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases
